4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Chaos To ClarityWhen I first started programming, I thought writing software meant writing functions.
See a problem.
Write a function.
Test it.
Move on.
If the output was correct and all the test cases passed, I considered the job done.
For a long time, this approach worked. It helped me solve coding problems, improve my Python skills, and get comfortable with algorithms. Like many aspiring software engineers, I spent countless hours practicing DSA and learning new programming concepts.
But everything changed when I started building larger Machine Learning projects and working with microservices.
That’s when I realized something uncomfortable.
Writing functions isn’t software engineering.
It’s only a small part of it.
My First Reality Check
The first few ML projects I built followed a familiar pattern.
I would create one function after another.
A function for preprocessing.
Another for model training.
One more for prediction.
Eventually, everything would be stitched together inside a huge main() function.
It worked.
Until it didn’t.
As projects grew, making even a small change meant tracing through dozens of functions. Debugging became frustrating. Reusing code became difficult. Every new feature felt like it was balancing on top of an increasingly fragile structure.
The code wasn’t necessarily bad.
My thinking was.
I was still approaching every problem like someone solving coding questions instead of someone building software.
The Moment Everything Changed
The biggest shift happened when I started working on larger projects, especially those involving microservices.
Suddenly, the conversation wasn’t about writing a function.
It became questions like:

• Should this service even be responsible for this task?

• How will different services communicate?

• What happens if one service fails?

• Can this component be reused later?

• How will this scale when the project grows?
None of these questions had anything to do with syntax.
They were system questions.
And I wasn’t used to thinking that way.
That was the moment I realized software engineering isn’t just about writing code.
It’s about designing solutions.
From Coding Problems to Engineering Problems
Earlier, whenever I saw a new problem, my brain immediately searched for code.
Now, it starts somewhere completely different.
Instead of asking,
“What function should I write?”
I ask,
“How should this system work?”
I first break the problem into independent parts.
I think about responsibilities.
I identify what should be separated.
I decide how components should communicate.
Only after that do I begin writing code.
Ironically, I now spend more time thinking before opening my editor.
And I finish projects faster because of it.
My Development Process Looks Completely Different Today
Today, when I start a new project, my workflow looks something like this:

• Understand the actual problem.

• Break it into independent components.

• Find the simplest responsibility for each component.

• Think about data flow.

• Decide how everything connects.

• Start implementing one module at a time.

• Test every piece before integrating it.
The coding part hasn’t become easier.
But the project has become easier to manage.
One Lesson That Changed Everything
If I could go back and give my younger self one piece of advice, it would be this:
Don’t try to fit a solution you’ve already learned.
Instead,
understand how the problem actually works.
For a long time, I tried matching every problem to something I already knew.
A familiar algorithm.
A design pattern.
A framework.
Now I spend more time understanding the problem itself.
Once the problem is clear, the solution usually becomes much more obvious.
DSA Isn’t Enough
One belief I’ve completely changed my mind about is this:
“DSA is enough to become a software engineer.”
Don’t get me wrong.
Data Structures and Algorithms are incredibly important.
They teach problem solving.
They help during interviews.
They improve logical thinking.
But software engineering begins where DSA ends.
Real projects introduce questions that interview problems rarely ask.
How do you organize code?
How do you make it maintainable?
How do multiple services interact?
How do you handle failures?
How do you make future changes easier?
Those are engineering problems.
And solving them requires a completely different mindset.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The shift from solving coding problems to designing software systems.Why This Matters Even More for AI
As I’m moving toward AI Engineering, I believe systems thinking matters even more than writing models.
Building an AI application isn’t just about training a model.
It’s about designing an entire ecosystem around it.
Data pipelines.
Model serving.
APIs.
Inference services.
Caching.
Monitoring.
Security.
Versioning.
Deployment.
The model is only one component.
The system is the real product.
That’s the kind of engineer I want to become.
I’m Still Learning
I don’t think I’ve mastered this mindset.
Not even close.
Every new project teaches me something I didn’t know before.
Every architecture diagram reminds me there’s always a better way to organize software.
But that’s exactly what excites me.
I’m no longer chasing the perfect function.
I’m learning to build better systems.
And I think that’s one of the biggest milestones in my journey from software engineer to AI engineer.

What was the biggest mindset shift in your own programming journey?
I’d genuinely love to hear the moment when the way you looked at software changed.
